images.caption: Figure 1:Illustration of ZEDA. ZEDA leverages the post-trained MoE to initialize the dynamic MoE (with zero-expert injection) and further utilizes it as a teacher model for distillation.
Figure 2:Inference efficiency comparison between the original MoE and ZEDA at81928192sequence length. Speedup is defined relative to the original MoE.
Figure 3:Distribution of token-levelrZ​Er_{ZE}versus teacher-student logp-diff (left) and entropy (right) for all generated tokens across 110 rollouts. Each point represents a single token.

Figure 4:Visualization of per-tokenrZ​Er_{ZE}for decoded text, showing one sampled response from AIME24 (left), LiveCodeBench v5 (middle), and IFBench (right), respectively. Due to space constraints, only the first and last 80 tokens of each response are retained.

Figure 5:Visualization ofrZ​Er_{ZE}across layers and response positions, using the same data in Figure4. Token-levelrZ​Er_{ZE}values are averaged over chunks of size 1000 (for AIME24 and LiveCodeBench v5) and 100 (for IFBench), respectively. The last chunk is averaged over its actual token number.

Figure 6:Scaling trend of average benchmark score andrZ​Er_{ZE}over different amounts of SFT data and adaptation time (left). Effect of the zero-expert group weight on average score andrZ​Er_{ZE}(right).
